macro_segments.title: Introduction and Prior Context: Me'ilah Be'mezid and Ma'aser Sheni Status

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and recap of yesterday's discussion on me'ilah be'mezid
Review of the debate on ma'aser sheni as mammon gavoah vs. mammon hedyot and the status of me'ilah

macro_segments.title: The Prohibition on Purchasing Animals with Ma'aser Sheni Money and the Underlying Rationales

micro_segments.title: The baraita rule: ain lokchim behema b'dmei ma'aser sheni—cannot purchase animals with ma'aser sheni money outside Jerusalem
Rashi's first explanation: only money or original fruit can be brought to Jerusalem
Tosafot's challenge to Rashi's first explanation with evidence of other food purchases
Rashi's second explanation: animal devaluation during transport to Jerusalem
Tosafot's third explanation: concern about delay leading to animal offspring also becoming ma'aser sheni

macro_segments.title: The Case of Unwitting Purchase with Ma'aser Sheni Money and Reversal of Sale

micro_segments.title: The rule for unwitting (shogeg) purchase: yachzeru damim limkomam—sale is reversed
Explanation based on Rabbi Yehuda's principle: mekach taos—had you known, you wouldn't have agreed

macro_segments.title: The Case of Intentional (Mezid) Purchase: When the Animal Acquires Ma'aser Sheni Kedusha

micro_segments.title: Rule for intentional purchase: bemezid ta'aleh vetachel bamakom—bring animal to Jerusalem with ma'aser sheni kedusha
Rabbi Yehuda's limitation: only if intentionally acquiring the animal for ma'aser sheni kedusha and consumption in Jerusalem
Natural use case: buying animals with ma'aser sheni to bring as shelamim sacrifice and eat in Jerusalem

macro_segments.title: The Contrast: Intentionally Transferring Ma'aser Sheni to Profane Use and Misuse Concerns

micro_segments.title: The problematic scenario: intentionally spending ma'aser sheni money to get profane (chulin) animals while sticking the seller with the money
Tosafot's reading: contrasting scenarios of intentional transfer of kedusha versus intentional diversion to profane use
The concern of zilzul ma'aser sheni: misuse risk and diversion from intended purpose
The solution: reverse the sale to protect ma'aser sheni money from misappropriation

macro_segments.title: Comparison with Kiddushin B'Ma'aser Sheni: Why Marriage Does Not Require Reversal

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah rule: if one intentionally uses ma'aser sheni to acquire a woman, the kiddushin is valid (Rabbi Yehuda)
The question: why don't we reverse the kiddushin as we reverse the animal sale, given the same misuse concerns?
Rabbi Elazar's answer: the woman (chavera) knows that ma'aser sheni money retains its kedusha and will use it properly

macro_segments.title: The Challenge: Non-Food Purchases with Ma'aser Sheni Money and the Kedusha Issue

micro_segments.title: Rav Yirmiyah's challenge: if one buys non-food items (tamei animals, slaves, land), kedusha cannot transfer, yet we might allow the transaction
The presumption: if the buyer knows the kedusha is retained, he should not misuse the money (parallel to woman case)
The Gemara response: even by non-food items, one cannot purchase with ma'aser sheni, but if purchased, an alternative remedy applies

macro_segments.title: The Alternative Remedy: Yochel K'Negdan When the Seller Cannot Be Reversed

micro_segments.title: The rule for non-food items: v'im lakach yochal k'negdan—buyer must spend equal money on food in Jerusalem
Two possible interpretations: whether the seller's money becomes chulin or the kedusha transfers to the buyer's subsequent spending

macro_segments.title: The Core Distinction: Why Reversal vs. Yochel K'Negdan Depends on Seller Presence and Circumstances, Not Object Type

micro_segments.title: The Gemara question: why use different solutions (reversal vs. yochel k'negdan) for different object types?
Samuel's answer: keshe'barach—the difference is whether the seller is present; if not present, yochel k'negdan applies
The decision chart: three conditions—Does buyer know? Is seller present? Determines reversal or yochel k'negdan

macro_segments.title: Why Reversal is Preferred: Taama D'Varach—The Rationale for Punishing the Seller

micro_segments.title: Question: why is reversal the default rather than yochel k'negdan, given both solutions exist?
Answer: if seller is present, we fine the seller (kansinan lemocher) to reverse the sale
Why fine the seller over the buyer: the seller is more eager to sell and benefits from offloading; reversal is the appropriate penalty

macro_segments.title: The Deeper Principle: Rectifying the Ma'aser Sheni Money's Location, Not Punishing Individuals

micro_segments.title: The counter-argument: lav achbara chura, without the seller as the 'hole,' the buyer would not have the opportunity; seller is enabling factor
The Gemara challenge: the buyer (who spent ma'aser sheni wrongly) seems more deserving of punishment than the seller
The resolution: mistavra—both are complicit, but the focus is on where the ma'aser sheni money currently is (with the seller); we rectify that situation, not punish

macro_segments.title: Transition to Issurei Hana'ah: Forbidden-Benefit Objects and Kiddushin Validity

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah introduction: moving from holy (kedosha) objects to forbidden-benefit (issurei hana'ah) objects—six categories listed
Key cases: orlah, kilai hakerem, shor haniskal, heifer rites, metzora birds, nazir hair, firstborn donkey, meat and milk
The Mishnah rule: hamikadesh b'orlah eino mekudeshet—if one acquires a woman with forbidden-benefit objects, the kiddushin is invalid
Distinction from kedusha: status of issurei hana'ah does not transfer to the money received (unlike kedusha)

macro_segments.title: Tosafot's Question on Kiddushin with Issurei Hana'ah and Multiple Answers

micro_segments.title: Tosafot's question: why is kiddushin invalid if some forbidden-benefit objects have minimal market value through non-standard use?
First Tosafot answer: if the forbidden-benefit object has shaveh prutah value even via non-standard use, kiddushin is valid
Second Tosafot answer: even with value, if woman is unaware of non-standard use limitation, it is mekach taos
Rashba's answer: by law, forbidden-benefit objects are valueless regardless of actual market price; kedusha cannot transfer to valueless items

macro_segments.title: Gemara Sources: Biblical and Rabbinic Proofs that Issurei Hana'ah Are Forbidden in Benefit

micro_segments.title: Ba'orlah minalan: orlah—from 'lo ye'achel' (do not eat) derives no benefit via 'v'arlat orlato' (make it completely off-limits)
Bekilai hakerem minalan: from 'pen tikdash' (lest it become sanctified) read as 'pen tukad esh' (lest it be burned), meaning completely forbidden
Rav Ashi's reading: pen yihyeh kodesh—it is like something holy; holy things are forbidden in benefit, so kilai are forbidden
Objection: kedusha can transfer to money and object becomes chulin; why not apply to kilai?
Resolution: prefer Chizkiyah's drasha of 'pen tukad esh' (deserving to be burned) over the 'kedusha' reading

macro_segments.title: Shor Haniskal: Forbidden Benefit via Slaughtered Meat and the Standard Interpretation of 'Lo Ye'achel'

micro_segments.title: Shor haniskal minalan: the ox stoned for goring is a neveilah (carcass), already forbidden to eat
The redundancy question: why does the Torah repeat 'lo ye'achel et besaro' if it's already a neveilah?
Interpretation: to teach that even if slaughtered after the judgment (like regular meat), it remains forbidden
How do we derive forbidden benefit (hana'ah)? From 'ba'al hashor naki'—owner is cleaned out, has no benefit left

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer's Principle: Standard 'Lo Ye'achel' Includes Forbidden Benefit Unless Explicitly Permitted

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer's principle: kol makom shene'emar 'lo ye'achel'—whenever the Torah says 'do not eat,' it includes both eating and benefit
Exception: unless the pasuk explicitly permits benefit, like by neveilah where you can sell to a non-Jew
Therefore shor haniskal: even though we knew it was assur b'achilah from the neveilah rule, the repeated 'lo ye'achel' adds the forbidden-benefit aspect
